title: Phase 1 -- 10 most surprising findings
subtitle: Six current multilingual LLMs (gpt-4o, claude-sonnet-4, gemini-2.0-flash, llama-3.3-70b, mistral-large-2411, qwen-2.5-72b) x three languages (EN, CS, JA) x three replicates of the burial-ban dilemma. 54 manually coded responses at temperature = 0.
headline: Language shifts the model's verdict more than temperature noise does, but only on what is recommended -- not on how agency is staged in the reply itself.

findings.title: Only one model is language-invariant
findings.summary: 5 of 6 models change their modal recommendation just because the prompt language changed -- identical facts, identical temperature, identical seed. Only mistral-large is invariant (compromise everywhere). gpt-4o, claude, gemini, llama, and qwen all shift their modal VAR-A across EN/CS/JA.
findings.evidence: modal_per_cell_var_a (rows: model, columns: EN/CS/JA, value: modal code)
findings.implication: Language alone, without any framing manipulation, moves the verdict in most current LLMs. The Phase 1 hypothesis (language = IV) is confirmed.

findings.title: Japanese is the most legalistic, not the most collectivist
findings.summary: 72% of all Japanese replies endorse legal_challenge (courts, dialogue with toukyoku, human-rights groups), versus 44% in Czech and 17% in English. Pre-registered intuition might say JA would pull toward affectedness or collective harmony; instead the model verdict is the most institutionally legalistic of the three.
findings.evidence: by_language.var_a -- ja: 13/18 legal_challenge, 3/18 undecided, 1 secret_bury, 1 compromise
findings.implication: When researchers expect 'cultural softness' in Japanese model output, they often get the opposite -- LLM Japanese reads like institutional pull, not communal yielding.

findings.title: Only Claude blames the deceased terrorist -- and only in English
findings.summary: Across 54 responses, only 3 cells code deceased as the primary responsibility locus (VAR-B). All three are Claude in English (P1-EN, all 3 replicates). The same Claude in Czech and Japanese behaves like every other model and blames authorities. This is Claude-specific moralizing that is itself language-conditional.
findings.evidence: by_model_language.var_b -- claude4 en: deceased x3; claude4 cs/ja: authorities
findings.implication: Model 'fingerprints' are language-conditional. A property of Claude may simply not exist when you query it in Czech.

findings.title: Llama in Japanese is the only cell that blames the relative
findings.summary: In 17 of 18 (model x language) cells, the reply stages authorities institutionally and assigns blame to authorities or distributed. The single exception: llama 3.3 in JA, which says 'kekka no sekinin wa shinzoku ni aru' -- the responsibility is on the relative. Two of three llama JA replicates code individualized / relative. No other model in any language does this.
findings.evidence: replicate_stability_var_a + by_model_language.var_b; llama3.3 ja r1,r3: relative; llama3.3 ja r2: distributed
findings.implication: Llama's tendency to wobble out of the institutional default appears specifically in Japanese -- which is the very language where everyone else converges hardest on institutional staging.

findings.title: Mistral at temperature 0 gave 3 different verdicts to the same Japanese prompt
findings.summary: Same prompt, same model, same seed parameter, temperature = 0. Three independent calls returned three different action codes: r1 compromise, r2 legal_challenge, r3 undecided. This is direct evidence that OpenRouter providers ignore the seed parameter for non-OpenAI models.
findings.implication: The deterministic guarantee of temperature = 0 is a polite fiction at the provider edge. Several 'replicates' in our other cells are also not really independent samples. Phase 2 will switch to temperature = 0.3 to surface real variance.

findings.title: Only 1 of 54 responses endorses secret burial
findings.summary: 54 calls, 5 possible action codes, secret_bury is the explicit second option in the prompt. Across 6 models x 3 languages x 3 replicates, only ONE reply endorses it: qwen 2.5 in Japanese, replicate 3 -- 'missou wo sentaku suru koto ga rinriteki ni seitouka sareru' (secret burial is ethically justified). 53/54 = 98% of LLMs refuse the disobedience branch.
findings.evidence: by_language.var_a -- ja secret_bury: 1; all other cells: 0
findings.implication: Current LLMs pre-empt the binary they were given. Even when offered defiance on equal footing, they re-route to mediation.

findings.title: Pure obedience is also nearly absent -- except in Czech Llama
findings.summary: Across 54 cells, 'obey' shows up exactly once: llama 3.3 P1-CS r3, which writes 'Pribuzna by se mela podridit zakazu'. The other two llama CS replicates code legal_challenge. Together with finding 6: the two explicitly offered options (obey, secret_bury) together account for ~4% of replies.
findings.implication: Models do not actually answer the binary they are asked. They reframe the problem into a third option (legal_challenge / compromise / undecided).

findings.title: Institutional staging is a universal cognitive default
findings.summary: Regardless of model and language, 89% or more of replies stage authorities as the actor (VAR-D = institutional). This is independent of what the reply then recommends. Even replies that endorse compromise or legal_challenge typically narrate 'the authorities must decide', 'the authorities are responsible'.
findings.implication: The grammar of the reply is more universal than its content. Variation in VAR-A (action) sits on top of a shared institutional frame. Phase 2 deagentive prompts (C2, E2) target exactly this default.

findings.title: English is the hedging language
findings.summary: When asked in English, models are most likely to refuse to commit. 33% of EN replies are coded 'undecided' (engages with both options but refuses to rank). CS = 0%, JA = 17%.
findings.implication: The institutional grammars of English -- 'balancing', 'no single right answer', 'it depends on values' -- give the model a culturally available exit that Czech does not.

findings.title: Japanese is 3-4x shorter and 100% flattens stance
findings.summary: Median response length across all 6 models: EN > CS > JA. Claude writes 1,632 chars in EN and 472 in JA (3.5x ratio) for the same content. And on VAR-R (Japanese register mirroring): 18 of 18 JA responses flatten the prompt's stance into plain analyst Japanese (de aru chou). No model mirrors any Japanese stance, evidential, or aspect marker.
findings.implication: Sets the Phase 2 baseline. If J3 (family POV), J5 (toukyoku reportative), J9 (you da ne shared stance) also produce 100% flattening, the flattening hypothesis is strongly supported.

phase2_implications.phase2_action: Test mistral on C2 deagentive and E2 deagentive -- does deagentive grammar finally break the language-invariance?
Run E2-E5 on Claude -- which English framing axis pushes Claude off the 'deceased did it' frame?
Run J3 (V2 family POV) and J4 (V3 adversative passive) on llama -- does it amplify or reverse the relative-blame finding?
Run all of Phase 2 at temperature = 0.3 (already wired via --temperature flag) to get genuine replicate variance.
Run C2 and E2 (deagentive). The missing agent may break the binary refusal -- does any model now endorse secret_bury when authorities are not named?
C4 (family affectedness, ethical dative) and J3 (family POV) test whether the institutional default holds when the prompt foregrounds the family.
J3, J5, J9 each carry a distinct Japanese stance marker. Mirroring rate (VAR-R != flattens_neutral) is the Phase 2 Japanese dependent measure.
